evidence_quotes.quote: the ever growing practice of publishers unilaterally revoking rights to (or disabling the functionality of) digital media such as movies, e-books, music tracks, video games, etc. that has been sold to the consumer without a clearly stated time of availability or any declared End-Of-Life plan.
state in the ToS that they reserved the rights to revoke access at any time for no reason

paragraphs.text: The Digital Fairness Act is a great initiative that I would really like to see come through. I would also like to propose to add another point to it: the ever growing practice of publishers unilaterally revoking rights to (or disabling the functionality of) digital media such as movies, e-books, music tracks, video games, etc. that has been sold to the consumer without a clearly stated time of availability or any declared End-Of-Life plan. The publishers argue, that they sell goods-as-service and state in the ToS that they reserved the rights to revoke access at any time for no reason, all while misinforming the consumers, who expect that they are purchasing the media as goods that would remain available (and functional) indefinitely. Please do consider this problem that is plaguing more and more consumers nowadays and include in the regulation the requirements for the publisher to clearly declare the temporal nature of the supplied digital media and work out the End-Of-Life plan if it is expected that the functionality will not be supported indefinitely. Thank you!
